title: Cortex Chess
description: A from-scratch chess engine AND analysis studio in TypeScript — PVS search with transposition tables, SEE, quiescence, null-move + late-move reductions, internal iterative reduction, countermoves and a tapered eval. Endgames are solved exactly in the browser by retrograde analysis: KPK/KRK/KQK plus the full King+Bishop+Knight vs King distance-to-mate table (~33.6M positions, the hardest elementary mate, built and exhaustively self-verified in seconds). A single material-generic retrograde solver derives the whole family of pawnless 3-4-man distance-to-mate tablebases — reproducing the hand-rolled KRK/KQK/KBNvK tables bit-for-bit and newly solving KBBvK (the two-bishop mate), KNNvK and the major-piece combinations via a DTM bucket queue with Syzygy-style sub-table probing; built tables persist to IndexedDB and warm instantly so the engine plays the ending perfectly. Plus UCI-style time management (clock + increment). It even has a from-scratch NNUE (efficiently-updatable neural-network) evaluation — no ML libraries — trained in your browser by knowledge distillation from the hand-crafted eval with a hand-rolled Adam SGD: its first layer is an incrementally-updated accumulator kept bit-for-bit in lock-step with make/unmake (proven against a full refresh), the hand-derived gradients are finite-difference checked, and the NNUE Lab trains it live with a loss curve, a net-vs-classical correlation scatter (R²≈0.92), and an 8-game head-to-head. It even ships a pre-trained default net (R²=0.99, trained offline with the repo's own trainer) so the NNUE eval works on a fresh load with no training — toggle it on to play the engine on a learned eval, or train and save your own to override it. And it ships the real-engine speed trick — fixed-point quantization: the net is lowered to an int16 feature transformer, a uint8 clipped-ReLU activation and an int8·uint8 output dot accumulated in int32 (the exact arithmetic a CPU's VNNI lanes run), the integer accumulator staying incrementally-updatable and bit-for-bit exact, proven in the Lab to track the float eval within a derived a-priori bound (mean ~3cp, 99% move agreement) at half the size, with an int8-vs-float head-to-head. Play with adjustable strength, fixed movetime and real pondering (instant ponder-hits); open the Analyze board to import a PGN, step through with a multi-PV engine, a whole-game evaluation graph, an opening explorer and one-click annotated-PGN export; or hit the Engine Lab to run perft, tactics, the Bratko-Kopec / Win-at-Chess EPD benchmark suites, the tablebase verifier and the NNUE trainer. It also plays full Chess960 / Fischer Random (and Double Fischer Random): all 960 Scharnagl start positions, general king-captures-rook castling on arbitrary files (X-FEN and Shredder-FEN), and an in-app self-test that proves it — the id⇄position bijection, an exact perft anchor through the 960 castle path, hash/make-unmake integrity over random 960 trees, and an independent castle-move oracle cross-checking tens of thousands of castles. And it now has a SECOND, independent search engine — a from-scratch AlphaZero-style PUCT Monte-Carlo Tree Search built on the same board and evaluation: each simulation descends by the PUCT rule (Q + c·P·√ΣN/(1+N)), reads a leaf value from the static/NNUE eval (no random rollouts, AlphaZero-style), and backs it up, with a choice of policy (a 1-ply-eval softmax or hand-crafted move features), eval-initialised first-play urgency, Dirichlet root noise and a selection temperature. An MCTS-Solver layer grafts exact win/loss/draw proofs with distance onto the statistics, so forced mates are found and reported precisely (proven in 1-14 simulations) rather than merely scored 'very winning'. A new MCTS tab lays the search bare: the live root visit distribution (the real policy a value-net search emits) as bars, the value and principal variation, a self-play mode, and a head-to-head against the alpha-beta searcher at an equal node budget — and the MCTS-Solver, the visit bookkeeping, the prior normalisation and PV legality all join the Self-tests Lab.
